idea.hook: What if a 'moon' was almost as big as its 'planet'?
facts: Pluto's largest 'moon', Charon, is 1,212 km across – over half of Pluto's 2,376 km diameter.
They are so close in mass that their combined gravitational center (barycenter) lies in the empty space BETWEEN them.
Rather than Charon orbiting Pluto, they both orbit this invisible point in space every 6.4 days, while tidally locked.
idea.payoff: This makes them the Solar System's only recognized binary dwarf planet system, a truly unique cosmic dance!
idea.title: Pluto & Charon: A Cosmic Duet! 🕺💃🪐

scenes.text: What if a 'moon' was nearly as big as its 'planet'?
scenes.visual: Abstract cosmic background. A large 'moon' silhouette slowly approaches a slightly larger 'planet' silhouette, filling the screen with a question mark.

scenes.text: Pluto (2376km) & Charon (1212km). Charon is >1/2 Pluto's size!
scenes.visual: Split screen or side-by-side comparison. Left: Pluto with '2376km' label. Right: Charon with '1212km' label, clearly showing its relative size. Both drift slowly.

scenes.text: Their masses are so close, their gravitational center is BETWEEN them.
scenes.visual: Pluto and Charon slowly rotating. A small, glowing, invisible point (barycenter) pulsates gently in the empty space between them, highlighted.

scenes.text: They BOTH orbit this barycenter every 6.4 days, tidally locked.
scenes.visual: Animated simulation showing both Pluto and Charon revolving around the central barycenter point, keeping the same face towards each other. Orbital paths faintly visible.

scenes.text: The ONLY binary dwarf planet system. A truly unique cosmic dance!
scenes.visual: A majestic, slow-motion shot of the Pluto-Charon system, gracefully 'dancing' together against a beautiful, deep space nebula background. Subtle lens flares or cosmic dust trails.
